segments.title: Imperial Russian destroyer numbers hid capability gaps
segments.summary: The episode argues that Imperial Russia often counted reclassified torpedo boats as destroyers, masking the shortage of capable screening ships its separated fleets needed.
segments.body: The discussion captures the lecture's organizing argument. Dr. Clarke distinguishes real destroyers from torpedo boats relabeled after the fact, ties the problem to Tsushima and Russia's separated fleets, and argues that later capable ships such as the Novik family arrived too late and in insufficient numbers to solve the strategic gap.

evidence.note: The opening frames the classification problem and explains why destroyers made strategic sense for Russia's separated fleets.
The Tsushima discussion says Russia needed destroyers to keep Japanese torpedo boats at bay but mostly had large torpedo boats later labeled as destroyers.
The discussion notes capable Russian destroyers existed or were coming, but were not ready or used when Tsushima made them most valuable.
The Novik and closing sections argue Russia eventually built powerful destroyers but not early enough or in enough numbers for its multiple fleets and wartime options.
